source.title: Accurately predicting mood episodes in mood disorder patients using wearable sleep and circadian rhythm features

confidence_rationale: Prospective longitudinal study, n=168, mean 587 days follow-up, strong AUCs (manic 0.98, hypomanic 0.95, depressive 0.80). The key predictive feature was daily circadian phase shift derived from wearable sleep-wake data -- an indirect actigraphy-derived measure rather than explicit staging. This is strongly relevant to Q-033's claim that wearable-derived latent-state indices can outperform simple total sleep time in predicting decompensation type. The episode-type-specific predictions (phase delay -> depression, phase advance -> mania) directly support Q-033's specificity subhypothesis. Confidence capped at 0.72: clinical population is mood disorder (not exclusively bipolar or cognitive), prediction horizon is next-day (Q-033 asks about longer-horizon decompensation timing), and the 'error burden' component is absent.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Wearable-derived circadian phase shifts predict next-day mood episodes with high accuracy (manic AUC 0.98, hypomanic 0.95, depressive 0.80); phase delays predict depressive episodes and phase advances predict manic episodes.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-033 specifically asks whether a sleep-phase sufficiency mismatch index can predict decompensation type with better specificity than total sleep time alone. This study demonstrates that episode-type-specific prediction is achievable from wearable circadian features -- and that the feature doing the work (phase direction of shift) is qualitatively different from total sleep time. This is a partial existence proof for the Q-033 specificity subhypothesis. The 'mismatch' in Q-033 (sleep-phase vs waking error burden) is a more complex construct than phase shift alone, but the directional mapping (phase delay -> depression, advance -> mania) suggests the sleep-phase signal carries episode-type information that TST does not.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The prediction horizon is one day ahead, not the multi-week clinical decompensation timing that Q-033 envisions. The model uses 36 derived circadian/sleep features from wearables but does not include a 'prediction error load estimate' from passive behavioural or physiological streams as Q-033 proposes. The 'mismatch index' in Q-033 is conceptually richer than circadian phase shift alone.
mapping.source_context: South Korean clinical cohort; 168 mood disorder patients (bipolar I/II and major depression); mean 587 days total observation; wearable sleep-wake monitoring ~267 days.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high: prospective, long follow-up, large sample for this field, npj Digital Medicine. Mapping fidelity moderate: directly demonstrates episode-type-specific prediction from wearable circadian features, which is the most relevant aspect of Q-033's specificity claim, but the error-burden mismatch index is absent. Transfer risk moderate-high: Korean clinical cohort, next-day prediction vs multi-week decompensation horizon, mood disorder vs broader psychiatric/cognitive Q-033 scope.
